criteria: The agent has read the Introduction to understand the iterative attractor algorithm concept: starting from a seed gene, it converges to a ranked gene co-expression signature.

The agent has read Section 2.1 completely to understand the five main steps of the CASCC algorithm: (1) graph-based initial clustering, (2) DEG selection as seeds, (3) adaptive attractor run and feature extraction, (4) determining K, (5) K-means with attractor-based initialization.
The agent has read Section 2.2 completely to understand the two evaluation dataset collections (15 benchmark datasets and 19 Tabula Sapiens datasets) and the evaluation metrics used.
The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the comparative results of CASCC versus six baseline methods across multiple datasets and metrics.
The agent has read Figure 1a to understand the overview of the CASCC clustering pipeline.
The agent has read Figure 1b to understand ARI performance of CASCC and six baselines on the 15 benchmark datasets, noting CASCC's 34% improvement over SC3.
The agent has read Figure 1c to understand ARI performance of CASCC and five baselines (SIMLR excluded) on the 19 large-scale Tabula Sapiens datasets.
The agent has read Supplementary Table S1 to check the identifiers, tissue sources, and cell counts of the 15 benchmark scRNA-seq evaluation datasets.
The agent has read Supplementary Table S2 to check the identifiers and sizes of the 19 Tabula Sapiens organ datasets (ranging from 5000 to 30000 cells).
The plan includes the overall CASCC R package architecture, covering the five-step pipeline, data loading utilities, baseline wrappers, and evaluation metric functions.
The plan includes downloading and loading the 15 evaluation datasets as SingleCellExperiment objects from the hemberg-lab scRNA-seq datasets repository.
The plan includes downloading and loading the 19 Tabula Sapiens human organ datasets from the figshare repository.
The plan includes implementing the initial low-computational-complexity graph-based clustering step using Seurat as the first step of CASCC.
The plan includes selecting top-ranked differentially expressed genes (DEGs) from each initial cluster to serve as features and as potential seeds for the adaptive attractor algorithm.
The plan includes implementing the adaptive attractor algorithm that takes seed genes, iteratively converges, and outputs a ranked list of co-expressed genes.
The plan includes extracting the top co-expressed genes from each resulting attractor to serve as additional features in the feature-selected expression matrix.
The plan includes determining the final number of clusters K based on the attractor output.
The plan includes implementing K-means clustering on the feature-selected expression matrix, using cells with the highest expression levels of attractors as the initial cluster centers.
The plan includes wrappers or scripts to run all six baseline methods (CIDR, RaceID3, SC3, Seurat V4, SIMLR, TSCAN) on the evaluation datasets.
The plan includes computing ARI, AMI, and NMI metrics to compare clustering results against ground-truth cell type labels.
The plan includes computing the absolute log-modulus and deviation score metrics to evaluate accuracy of estimating the number of cell types.
The plan includes computing the average silhouette width (ASW) on 2D embeddings produced by each method.
The plan includes a parallel computing option to reduce the computation time of CASCC.
The plan includes a Wilcoxon test to assess statistical significance of CASCC's ARI improvement over each baseline method.
The code for loading the 15 evaluation datasets as SingleCellExperiment objects from the hemberg-lab repository has been implemented.
The code for loading the 19 Tabula Sapiens organ datasets from figshare has been implemented.
The code for data preprocessing (e.g., normalization and log transformation of the raw count matrix) prior to clustering has been implemented.
The code for the initial graph-based clustering step using Seurat V4 (e.g., FindNeighbors and FindClusters) as step 1 of CASCC has been implemented.
The code for identifying and selecting top-ranked differentially expressed genes (DEGs) from each initial cluster as candidate features and seeds has been implemented.
The code for the adaptive attractor algorithm — starting from each seed gene, iteratively computing co-expression, and converging to a ranked gene signature — has been implemented.
The code for the iterative convergence mechanism of the adaptive attractor algorithm, ensuring it terminates and outputs a stable ranked gene list, has been implemented.
The code for selecting the top co-expressed genes from each attractor output to augment the feature set for downstream clustering has been implemented.
The code for constructing the feature-selected expression matrix by combining top DEGs and top attractor genes has been implemented.
The code for determining the final number of clusters K based on the attractor algorithm output (e.g., the number of distinct attractors found) has been implemented.
The code for K-means clustering performed on the feature-selected expression matrix has been implemented.
The code for selecting initial K-means cluster centers as the cells with the highest expression levels of each attractor has been implemented.
The code for computing dimensionality reduction (PCA) as part of the preprocessing pipeline for CASCC has been implemented.
The code for generating 2D embeddings (t-SNE or UMAP) for visualization and ASW evaluation has been implemented.
The code for computing the Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) between predicted cluster labels and ground-truth cell type labels has been implemented.
The code for computing Adjusted Mutual Information (AMI) between predicted and true labels has been implemented.
The code for computing Normalized Mutual Information (NMI) between predicted and true labels has been implemented.
The code for computing the absolute log-modulus metric to evaluate accuracy of the estimated number of cell types has been implemented.
The code for computing the deviation score to evaluate accuracy of the estimated number of cell types has been implemented.
The code for computing the average silhouette width (ASW) on 2D embeddings has been implemented.
The code for running the Wilcoxon signed-rank test to compare CASCC's ARI scores with those of each baseline method has been implemented.
The code for running the CIDR baseline method on the evaluation datasets has been implemented.
The code for running the RaceID3 baseline method on the evaluation datasets has been implemented.
The code for running the SC3 baseline method on the evaluation datasets has been implemented.
The code for running the Seurat V4 baseline method on the evaluation datasets has been implemented.
The code for running the SIMLR baseline method on the 15 small evaluation datasets (not applied to large datasets) has been implemented.
The code for running the TSCAN baseline method on the evaluation datasets has been implemented.
The code for the parallel computing option in CASCC that reduces computational time has been implemented.
The CASCC method has been packaged as an R package with proper exported functions callable by users.
The code for CASCC has been executed successfully on all 15 benchmark evaluation datasets without errors.
The code for all six baseline methods (CIDR, RaceID3, SC3, Seurat V4, SIMLR, TSCAN) has been executed on all 15 benchmark datasets without errors.
The code for CASCC has been executed on all 19 large-scale Tabula Sapiens datasets without errors.
The code for the five applicable baseline methods (CIDR, RaceID3, SC3, Seurat V4, TSCAN; SIMLR excluded) has been executed on all 19 Tabula Sapiens datasets without errors.
The ARI, AMI, and NMI scores for all methods on the 15 benchmark datasets have been computed and recorded.
The absolute log-modulus and deviation score for the estimated number of clusters by all methods have been computed and recorded.
The ASW values for 2D embeddings produced by each method have been computed and recorded.
The Wilcoxon test comparing CASCC's ARI scores against each baseline across the 15 datasets has been executed.
The execution outcomes match Figure 1b: CASCC achieves the highest median ARI on the 15 benchmark datasets compared to all six baseline methods.
The execution outcomes match the reported 34% improvement in mean ARI of CASCC over SC3 (the second-best method) on the 15 benchmark datasets.
The execution outcomes match Supplementary Figure S1: CASCC is the top performer on the AMI metric across the 15 benchmark datasets.
The execution outcomes match Supplementary Figure S1: CASCC is the top performer on the NMI metric across the 15 benchmark datasets.
The execution outcomes match Figure 1c: CASCC achieves the best ARI performance on the 19 large-scale Tabula Sapiens datasets compared to the five applicable baseline methods.
The execution outcomes match Supplementary Table S4: CASCC achieves statistically significant ARI differences compared to all six baseline methods as determined by the Wilcoxon test.
The execution outcomes match Supplementary Table S5: CASCC has the smallest absolute log-modulus value among all methods, indicating the most accurate estimation of the number of cell types.
The execution outcomes match Supplementary Table S5: CASCC has the smallest absolute deviation among all methods in estimating the number of cell types.
The execution outcomes match Supplementary Table S5: SC3 has the highest mean absolute deviation value in large datasets, indicating it significantly overestimates the number of cell types.
The execution outcomes match Supplementary Figure S2: CASCC and Seurat achieve much higher ASW values than the other methods on 2D embeddings, indicating better cluster separation and cohesion.

The execution outcomes confirm that SIMLR encounters out-of-memory errors when applied to the large-scale Tabula Sapiens datasets (at least 400 GB memory), consistent with its exclusion from the large-dataset comparison.
The execution outcomes match Supplementary Table S6: the computational complexity of CASCC is at a reasonable level compared to the other methods.
